Notable Events: Originated from 38 separate fires caused by lightning strikes, main fire (Doe Fire) surpassed 2018 Mendocino Complex as largest wildfire in California history, merged with Elkhorn and Hopkins fires, burned within Mendocino National Forest, took almost three months to fully contain, managed as four separate incidents within a larger complex
Records/Rankings: Single-largest wildfire and largest fire complex in recorded California history, burned an area larger than the state of Rhode Island
Locations Affected: Glenn, Lake, Mendocino, Tehama, Trinity, Shasta Counties, California

Notable Events: Second fire to cross Sierra Nevada mountain range in 2021, threatened Meyers and South Lake Tahoe, caused evacuations for over 20,000 people, third-largest and second-most-destructive wildfire of 2021 season in California, 15th-largest and 16th-most destructive in recorded California history
Records/Rankings: Third-largest and second-most-destructive wildfire of 2021 season in California, 15th-largest and 16th-most destructive in recorded California history
Locations Affected: El Dorado, Amador, Alpine County, California

Notable Events: Sixth-most destructive fire in California history, twelfth-largest wildfire in modern California history, cost over $1.659 billion in damages, destroyed multiple towns around Whiskeytown Lake, started by sparks from a vehicle's flat tire, jumped the Sacramento River into Redding, caused evacuations of 38,000 people, eight fatalities including three firefighters
Records/Rankings: Sixth-most destructive fire in California history, twelfth-largest wildfire in modern California history
Possible Cause: Sparks from a vehicle's flat tire

Notable Events: Rapid spread driven by Santa Ana winds, destructive nature, hotspots burned until December 5, 2003, one of the largest wildfires in California history, fifth deadliest and fourth-most destructive wildfire in state history, caused over $1.3 billion in damages
Records/Rankings: Eighth-largest wildfire in California's modern history

Notable Events: Fifth-largest wildfire in modern California history, second-largest single fire not part of a greater complex, necessitated rescue of hundreds of people by National Guard helicopters, evacuations in various California towns, creation of a pyrocumulonimbus cloud, characterized as a plume-dominated blaze with extreme fire behavior and fire tornadoes observed
Records/Rankings: Fifth-largest wildfire in modern California history, second-largest single fire not part of a greater complex

Notable Events: Largest single wildfire in recorded California history, second-largest wildfire overall, damaged or destroyed towns like Greenville, Canyondam, Warner Valley, first fire to burn across Sierra Nevada crest, caused unhealthy air quality across Western US, most expensive US wildfire at $637.4 million, possibly started by PG&E equipment
Records/Rankings: Largest single wildfire in recorded California history, second-largest wildfire overall
Locations Affected: Butte, Plumas, Lassen, Shasta, Tehama Counties, California

Notable Events: Largest wildfire of the 2008 California wildfire season, originated as 11 separate wildfires before merging into a single massive complex fire, lasted over 3 months, progenitor fires caused by lightning, 10th largest fire in modern California history, two firefighters killed
Records/Rankings: 10th largest fire in modern California history

Notable Events: Complex of numerous lightning-sparked fires, Hennessey Fire merged with other fires, burned in Wine Country area, destroyed 1,491 structures, fourth-largest wildfire in California history
Locations Affected: Lake, Napa, Sonoma, Solano, Yolo Counties, California

Notable Events: Named for Matilija Creek, one of the largest known wildfires in California history until the 2003 Cedar Fire, currently the fourteenth-largest in the state's modern history, area had not seen a major fire since at least 1911, severe drought conditions prior to 1932, started near Ojai, possible causes include a hunter's campfire or an exploding tank of butane gas
Records/Rankings: One of the largest known wildfires in California history until the 2003 Cedar Fire, currently the fourteenth-largest in the state's modern history
Locations Affected: Santa Barbara National Forest, Southern California

Notable Events: Consisted of River Fire and Ranch Fire, Ranch Fire was California's single-largest recorded wildfire at the time, Ranch Fire burned 410,203 acres, collectively contained on January 4, 2019, destroyed 280 structures, caused at least $257 million in damages
Records/Rankings: Ranch Fire was California's single-largest recorded wildfire at the time, surpassed previous largest wildfires in California history
Locations Affected: Mendocino, Lake, Colusa, Glenn Counties, California

Notable Events: Started by lightning strike, evacuation orders for multiple communities, Highway 299 closure, fire scar of 2008 Cedar Fire, spot fires established on north side of Trinity River, power lines de-energized by PG&E
Locations Affected: Big Flat, Big Bar, Del Loma, Cedar Flat, Trinity County, California

Notable Events: 21 fires started by lightning, towns of Berry Creek and Feather Falls leveled, city of Oroville threatened, 16 fatalities, managed by U.S. Forest Service and Cal Fire, deadliest fire in the 2020 California wildfire season
Records/Rankings: Sixth-largest wildfire in California's modern history

Notable Events: Second-largest wildfire in California's history, fifth-largest modern wildfire in California as of 2018, largest wildfire on record in the Sierra Nevada mountain range, fully contained on October 24, 2013, caused by a hunter's illegal fire near the Rim of the World vista point, suppression efforts cost over $127 million, 11 residences, 3 commercial structures, and 98 outbuildings destroyed, 10 injuries reported, no fatalities
Records/Rankings: Second-largest wildfire in California's history, largest wildfire on record in the Sierra Nevada mountain range
Locations Affected: Stanislaus National Forest, Tuolumne and Mariposa counties, California

State of Emergency: Declared by Governor Jerry Brown on September 11, 2015
Comparison to Previous Seasons: Most destructive fire season since 2011, surpassed 2014 land area burned
Notable Incidents: Firefighter deaths due to carbon monoxide poisoning and falling tree, civilian death in Valley Fire

Notable Events: Largest wildfire of the 2012 California wildfire season, second-largest wildfire in California since 1932 at the time, surpassed by the Thomas Fire in 2017 and Ranch Fire in the Mendocino Complex Fire in 2018, destroyed habitat for greater sage-grouse and a single barn
Records/Rankings: Second-largest wildfire in California since 1932 at the time
Locations Affected: Lassen County, California; Washoe County, Nevada

Notable Events: Massive wildfire in California, burned parts of Orange, Riverside, and San Diego Counties, possibly the largest wildfire in California until 2018, severe drought conditions, katabatic wind events, high temperatures, multiple significant fires in the region
Records/Rankings: Possibly the largest wildfire in California until 2018

Notable Events: Fourth-largest overall wildfire in California's modern history, consisted of fires in multiple counties, reached Lick Observatory, fully contained on October 1, 2020
Records/Rankings: Fourth-largest overall wildfire in California's modern history
Locations Affected: Diablo Range in California, Santa Clara, Alameda, Contra Costa, San Joaquin, Merced, Stanislaus Counties

Notable Events: Largest wildfire in modern California history at the time, seventh-largest wildfire in modern California history as of 2021, officially declared out on June 1, 2018, destroyed at least 1,063 structures, caused over $2.2 billion in damages, seventh-most destructive wildfire in state history at the time, tenth-most destructive wildfire in California as of August 2020, Ventura's agriculture industry suffered at least $171 million in losses, cost over $204 million to fight by January 2, 2018, forced over 104,607 residents to evacuate, over 8,500 firefighters mobilized to fight it, began north of Santa Paula, near Steckel Park and south of Thomas Aquinas College, reached the city of Ventura, destroyed over five hundred residences in Ventura, threatened the Ojai Valley, completely surrounded the area including Lake Casitas
Records/Rankings: Seventh-largest wildfire in modern California history as of 2021, tenth-most destructive wildfire in California as of August 2020
Locations Affected: Ventura and Santa Barbara Counties, California

Notable Events: Second-largest wildfire of the 2007 California wildfire season, largest of the October 2007 California wildfires, merged with Poomacha and McCoy Fires, rapid spread due to Santa Ana winds, led to mass evacuations of 500,000 people, contributed to the largest evacuation in California history
Records/Rankings: Largest complex fire of 2007, exhibited characteristics of a firestorm, led to 1,000,000 residents evacuating
Locations Affected: Santa Ysabel, San Diego County, California

Notable Events: Second-largest fire in California history at the time, tenth-largest recorded fire in modern California history as of 2020, caused by sparks from a grinding machine, primarily burned in San Rafael Mountains, contained on October 29, 2007
Records/Rankings: Second-largest fire in California history at the time, tenth-largest recorded fire in modern California history as of 2020
Locations Affected: San Rafael Mountains, Santa Barbara County, California
Fatalities: 43 non-fatal injuries, 2 from a helicopter crash
